stage.name: Precise exceptions: why commit becomes the convergence point
stage.description: A precise-exception lesson that connects exceptions, interrupts, CSR trap state, commit ordering, and frontend restart behavior.
stage.courseTemplateName: Professional Technical Deep Dive - Evidence-Grounded SlideKit

scenes.title: Precise exceptions are a commit story

content.title: Precise exceptions: why commit becomes the convergence point
content.subtitle: The design text says exceptions are considered during commit and associated with the related instruction.
content.thesis: A precise trap is not just an error signal. It is a promise that the machine can explain exactly where architectural state stopped.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE is the last pipeline stage and updates architectural state by writing CSR registers, committing stores, and writing back data to the register file.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Traps, Interrupts and Exceptions
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual distinguishes synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts and lists trap-related CSR state such as mtvec, mepc, mcause, and mtval.

content.speakerNotes: Precise exception sounds like a specialist phrase, but the idea is friendly. When something goes wrong, or when an interrupt is taken, the machine must make a clean promise: all older architectural effects are handled, younger wrong-path effects are not visible, and the trap is associated with the right instruction. That is why commit is the convergence point.

actions.text: Precise exception sounds like a specialist phrase, but the idea is friendly. When something goes wrong, or when an interrupt is taken, the machine must make a clean promise: all older architectural effects are handled, younger wrong-path effects are not visible, and the trap is associated with the right instruction. That is why commit is the convergence point.

content.title: Use the RISC-V trap words carefully
content.subtitle: The user manual separates asynchronous interrupts from synchronous exceptions.

cards.title: Trap
cards.body: The umbrella event that transfers control to a trap handler.

cards.title: Exception
cards.body: A synchronous event associated with instruction execution.

cards.title: Interrupt
cards.body: An asynchronous event such as external, software, timer, or debug request.

cards.title: Trap CSRs
cards.body: CSRs such as mtvec, mepc, mcause, and mtval record handler target and cause state.

content.takeaway: Precise pipeline behavior and privileged CSR state are two halves of the same story.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Traps, Interrupts and Exceptions
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual distinguishes synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts and lists trap-related CSR state such as mtvec, mepc, mcause, and mtval.

content.speakerNotes: Before reading signals, get the vocabulary right. The manual distinguishes exceptions from interrupts. Exceptions are synchronous: they relate to instruction execution. Interrupts are asynchronous: they arrive from outside the sequential instruction stream. Trap CSRs then record where to go and what happened. Without this vocabulary, precise exception discussion becomes foggy very quickly.

actions.text: Before reading signals, get the vocabulary right. The manual distinguishes exceptions from interrupts. Exceptions are synchronous: they relate to instruction execution. Interrupts are asynchronous: they arrive from outside the sequential instruction stream. Trap CSRs then record where to go and what happened. Without this vocabulary, precise exception discussion becomes foggy very quickly.

content.title: The exception decision waits for architectural order
content.subtitle: The design text says exceptions are considered during commit and associated with the related instruction.

steps.title: Candidate event appears
steps.body: An exception may originate before commit, or from CSR_REGFILE or interrupt handling.

steps.title: Instruction reaches commit
steps.body: The machine now knows which instruction is at the retirement boundary.

steps.title: Trap state is selected
steps.body: CSR trap state can record PC, cause, and related information.

steps.title: Pipeline is redirected
steps.body: Flush and restart behavior prevents younger wrong-path effects from becoming real.

content.takeaway: Precision comes from associating the trap with the commit boundary, not from shouting earlier.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE is the last pipeline stage and updates architectural state by writing CSR registers, committing stores, and writing back data to the register file.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: CSR_REGFILE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: CSR_REGFILE interacts with commit, trap, return, and frontend redirection through CSR exception, EPC, trap-vector, and return signals.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 deprecated design docs: Commit stage
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The legacy commit-stage note states the design rule that no other pipeline stage updates architectural state under any circumstances.

content.speakerNotes: An exception can be detected before commit, but precise handling waits for the architectural boundary. At commit, the processor knows which instruction is being retired or faulted. CSR trap state can be updated with the right PC and cause. Then flush and redirect logic can remove younger work. The important part is not only detecting the event; it is associating it with the right architectural moment.

actions.text: An exception can be detected before commit, but precise handling waits for the architectural boundary. At commit, the processor knows which instruction is being retired or faulted. CSR trap state can be updated with the right PC and cause. Then flush and redirect logic can remove younger work. The important part is not only detecting the event; it is associating it with the right architectural moment.

scenes.title: Exceptions and interrupts meet the same discipline

content.title: Synchronous and asynchronous events still need ordered handling
content.subtitle: Different origins, same need for a precise architectural boundary.

columns.title: Exception
columns.body: A synchronous event tied to instruction execution, such as an illegal instruction or access fault.

columns.title: Interrupt
columns.body: An asynchronous event such as external, software, timer, or debug request that must still enter state cleanly.
points: May be delayed to a precise point

content.takeaway: The origin differs; the architectural cleanliness requirement does not.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Traps, Interrupts and Exceptions
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual distinguishes synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts and lists trap-related CSR state such as mtvec, mepc, mcause, and mtval.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE is the last pipeline stage and updates architectural state by writing CSR registers, committing stores, and writing back data to the register file.

content.speakerNotes: Exceptions and interrupts start differently. An exception is tied to an instruction event. An interrupt can arrive from outside the instruction stream. But both need clean architectural handling. The machine must not half-retire a younger instruction, apply the wrong store, or lose the PC context. That is why the commit boundary and CSR trap state are so tightly connected.

actions.text: Exceptions and interrupts start differently. An exception is tied to an instruction event. An interrupt can arrive from outside the instruction stream. But both need clean architectural handling. The machine must not half-retire a younger instruction, apply the wrong store, or lose the PC context. That is why the commit boundary and CSR trap state are so tightly connected.

scenes.title: How to explain precise exceptions without overclaiming

content.title: A precise-exception explanation needs four checks
content.subtitle: Use these checks before making public technical claims.
items: Name whether the event is a synchronous exception or asynchronous interrupt.
Identify the instruction or architectural boundary associated with the event.
Name the CSR trap state involved, such as EPC, cause, trap vector, or trap value.
Explain how younger work is flushed or prevented from becoming visible.
Avoid implying that every early detection immediately updates architectural state.
content.takeaway: Precise exception teaching is mostly disciplined wording plus the right source anchors.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE is the last pipeline stage and updates architectural state by writing CSR registers, committing stores, and writing back data to the register file.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Traps, Interrupts and Exceptions
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual distinguishes synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts and lists trap-related CSR state such as mtvec, mepc, mcause, and mtval.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: CONTROLLER module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The controller receives resolved branch and commit flush information and drives frontend and pipeline flush or restart control.

content.speakerNotes: This checklist is the practical tool. Name exception versus interrupt. Identify the instruction or commit boundary. Name the trap CSR role: EPC, cause, trap vector, or trap value. Then explain how younger work is flushed or prevented from becoming visible. If you skip any one of these, the lesson may sound confident but leave the learner with the wrong state model.

actions.text: This checklist is the practical tool. Name exception versus interrupt. Identify the instruction or commit boundary. Name the trap CSR role: EPC, cause, trap vector, or trap value. Then explain how younger work is flushed or prevented from becoming visible. If you skip any one of these, the lesson may sound confident but leave the learner with the wrong state model.

questions.question: What does "precise" mainly promise in this lesson?

options.label: The machine can explain the architectural boundary and related instruction for the trap
The frontend predictor is always correct
Stores commit before instruction order matters

questions.analysis: Precise handling is about clean architectural order and correct association with the trap event.

questions.question: Which event is synchronous and instruction-related?
questions.analysis: The manual separates synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts.

questions.question: Which CSR-related items belong in a trap-state explanation?
questions.analysis: Trap CSRs record handler target, saved PC, cause, and related trap values.

questions.question: Why is commit a natural place for precise exception convergence?

options.label: It is where architectural state is updated in order

questions.analysis: Commit is the architectural-state boundary, so it is the right place to associate precise trap handling.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE is the last pipeline stage and updates architectural state by writing CSR registers, committing stores, and writing back data to the register file.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Traps, Interrupts and Exceptions
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual distinguishes synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts and lists trap-related CSR state such as mtvec, mepc, mcause, and mtval.

content.speakerNotes: The quiz checks the state model. Precise does not mean early, loud, or mysterious. It means ordered and attributable. If you can separate exception and interrupt, name trap CSR roles, and connect the event to the commit boundary, you now have a serious foundation for reading backend control state.

actions.text: The quiz checks the state model. Precise does not mean early, loud, or mysterious. It means ordered and attributable. If you can separate exception and interrupt, name trap CSR roles, and connect the event to the commit boundary, you now have a serious foundation for reading backend control state.

scenes.title: Precise exceptions are ordered state repair

content.title: Precise means attributable and ordered
takeaways: Exceptions are synchronous; interrupts are asynchronous, but both need clean trap handling.
Commit gives the machine a reliable architectural boundary for association and state update.
Trap CSRs and flush/restart behavior explain how control transfers without corrupting state.
content.nextStep: Next lesson: Unit 06 checkpoint, where you classify execute, commit, branch, CSR, and exception cases.
content.closing: A precise trap is the processor saying: I know exactly where reality stopped.

sourceAnchors.title: CV32A60X design: COMMIT_STAGE module
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: COMMIT_STAGE is the last pipeline stage and updates architectural state by writing CSR registers, committing stores, and writing back data to the register file.

sourceAnchors.title: CVA6 user manual: Traps, Interrupts and Exceptions
sourceAnchors.claimSupported: The user manual distinguishes synchronous exceptions from asynchronous interrupts and lists trap-related CSR state such as mtvec, mepc, mcause, and mtval.

content.speakerNotes: That closes the precise exception lesson. Exceptions and interrupts enter from different directions, but both need ordered state repair. Commit gives us the boundary; trap CSRs give us the recorded context; flush and restart behavior clean up younger work. Next comes the checkpoint, where we test the entire execute-to-commit spine.
